name: Alice Mine near Elijah
short_description: Historic Alice Mine in the Missouri Ozarks offers rockhounding for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Explore abandoned mining areas on relatively accessible flat terrain.
description:
The Alice Mine near Elijah sits in the heart of the Missouri Ozarks, a region known for its historic lead and mineral mining heritage. This location provides rockhounders with access to quartz veins, feldspar crystals, and mica sheets that formed within metamorphic bedrock typical of the Ozark Dome geological structure. The site's flat elevation and modest terrain make it accessible to collectors of varying experience levels, though visitors should always respect abandoned mine safety protocols.

The geology here reflects the Proterozoic-age igneous and metamorphic rocks that underlie the Ozark region. Quartz veins were emplaced during periods of hydrothermal activity, and feldspar megacrysts can be found in granitic outcrops. Mica specimens, particularly muscovite, are commonly recovered from pegmatite zones and weathered rock exposures.

Expect to find small to medium-sized specimens scattered across mine tailings and exposed bedrock. The relatively flat surrounding terrain means less strenuous hiking than other Ozark sites, making this an ideal destination for families and newer rockhounders. Bring sturdy digging tools and containers, as specimens often require some extraction from host rock.
history: The Alice Mine was worked historically for lead and other minerals during Missouri's mining boom era. The Ozark region's metamorphic bedrock, formed during Proterozoic mountain-building events, hosts numerous mineral deposits that attracted prospectors and small mining operations throughout the 19th and 20th centuries.
tips: Search the mine tailings and rock piles first - these concentrated areas yield the highest density of quartz and feldspar specimens. Use a pry bar and hammer to carefully extract crystals from partially exposed pockets in the exposed bedrock along old mine cuts. Look for glassy quartz crystal clusters and shiny feldspar faces reflecting sunlight; mica will appear as thin, reflective sheets in weathered granite fragments.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 959 feet elevation with gentle slope. The site is characterized by disturbed mine workings, tailings piles, and exposed granitic bedrock. Walking distance from parking to productive areas is typically under half a mile. Ground footing is uneven due to old mine rubble and rock debris, requiring sturdy footwear. Sparse Ozark forest vegetation provides partial shade.
written_directions: From West Plains, take US 160 east toward Elijah approximately 12 miles. Turn north on County Road 5133 (or local access road toward mine area) and proceed about 2 miles to mine locality. GPS coordinates will be essential for final approach. Road conditions may be rough or seasonal.
road_conditions: County roads may be unpaved or rough depending on season. High-clearance vehicle recommended for final approach.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking near mine entrance. No developed lot. Respect private property boundaries and any posted access restrictions.
collection_rules: This is private land. Rockhounders must obtain permission from the property owner before collecting. Respect all posted signs and boundaries. Collection of specimens from abandoned mines carries inherent risk; do not enter unstable mine shafts or tunnels. Remove only surface specimens and tailings material. Leave the site in good condition.
rules:
Permission from property owner required
No entry into mine shafts or tunnels
Collect only from surface and tailings
Respect all posted signs and boundaries
No commercial collection
nearest_services: West Plains, Missouri approximately 12 miles west. Full services including gas, food, and water available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with uneven footing due to mine tailings and rock debris. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Limited walking distance from parking to productive areas (under half mile). Suitable for families and those with moderate mobility.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Alice Mine near Elijah, Missouri?
faq.answer: Alice Mine yields quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica sheets. Quartz typically appears as glassy crystal clusters in veins, feldspar as larger shiny faces in granitic rock, and mica as thin reflective sheets in weathered specimens. Specimens range from small to medium size.

faq.question: Is Alice Mine free to rockhound, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Alice Mine is on private land and requires permission from the property owner before collecting. There is no fee or formal permit system, but you must obtain landowner approval. Always check current access status before visiting.

faq.question: Can beginners find good specimens at Alice Mine in Missouri?
faq.answer: Yes, Alice Mine is beginner-friendly. The flat, easily accessible terrain and abundant tailings piles make it ideal for new rockhounders. No advanced equipment or skills required - a hammer and pry bar are sufficient for extracting specimens from exposed bedrock and mine waste.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect at Alice Mine near Elijah?
faq.answer: Bring a pry bar, geological hammer, chisel, and heavy-duty gloves. A collection bag or bucket helps carry specimens. Sturdy hiking boots are essential due to uneven mine tailings. A geologist's loupe is helpful for identifying feldspar and mica in host rock.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Alice Mine, Missouri?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best conditions. Spring and fall temperatures are moderate, summer is warm, and winter may bring wet conditions that make mine tailings muddy and hazardous. Roads may be impassable after heavy rain, so avoid winter months.

faq.question: How far is Alice Mine from West Plains, and are there nearby services?
faq.answer: Alice Mine is approximately 12 miles east of West Plains via US 160. West Plains has full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. Plan for a 20-30 minute drive from town. The remote location means you should bring supplies and fuel up before heading out.
